assets.name: lecture_05_figure_01.png
assets.rationale: None of the candidates is useful for note writing: they are all opening campus or title-card frames with no blackboard mathematics, diagram, or classroom layout. Candidate 04 is the clearest and most settled frame in the window, but this subtitle moment should still be skipped.

assets.name: lecture_05_figure_02.png
assets.subtitle_excerpt: And l times m have exactly the same action on any vector, and therefore m times l is equal to l times m as operators.
assets.caption_hint: And l times m have exactly the same action on any vector, and therefore m times l is equal to l times m as operators.

assets.name: lecture_05_figure_03.png
assets.rationale: All four candidates show the same board close-up, but the final frame is the most settled and complete. The full difference-quotient equation is cleanly visible, with the denominator and right-hand side clearly written, making it the most useful frame for reconstructing the step that becomes the time derivative of the state vector.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: We get the time derivative of the state vector of the system.

assets.name: lecture_05_figure_04.png
assets.subtitle_excerpt: If you know what the Hamiltonian is as an operator or as a matrix,
assets.caption_hint: If you know what the Hamiltonian is as an operator or as a matrix,

assets.name: lecture_05_figure_05.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the best compromise between completeness and occlusion. The top identification of the basis vectors with energy eigenvectors is still visible, and the lower expansion of the state as a sum over basis vectors is more legible here than in the earlier frames, where Susskind's arm blocks more of the equation. The final frame adds more body and eraser occlusion without revealing additional content.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: Let's go back to the original equation that the state vector is a sum over the eigenvectors.

assets.name: lecture_05_figure_06.png
assets.subtitle_excerpt: the z component in the Hamiltonian, but I want to find out how all the components, sigma x, sigma y, and sigma z, how they vary with time.
